name: Manasa
shortDescription: Hindu goddess of snakes, worshipped to prevent snakebites and cure snake poisoning.
description: Manasa, also known as Vishahari, is a prominent Hindu goddess primarily worshipped in Bengal and other parts of North and Northeast India. She is revered as the goddess of snakes, and her devotees seek her blessings to protect themselves from snakebites and cure snake poisoning. Manasa's origins are complex, blending tribal and Brahmanical traditions. Despite facing initial resistance from orthodox Brahmins, Manasa gradually gained acceptance and became an integral part of the Hindu pantheon. Her legends often portray her as a powerful and assertive figure who overcomes adversity and establishes her rightful place among the gods. She is considered both benevolent and fearsome, capable of both bestowing blessings and inflicting punishment.
subtitle: Serpent Queen, Vanquisher of Poison
role: Goddess of Snakes, protector from snakebites

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Manasa's origin stories vary. Some accounts state she was created from the mind of the sage Kashyapa, while others claim she is the daughter of Shiva. Her early life was fraught with challenges. She faced rejection from her husband, Jaratkaru, due to his ascetic vows, and indifference from her father, Shiva, and stepmother, Parvati. A pivotal myth involves the merchant Chand Sadagar, a staunch devotee of Shiva, who refused to acknowledge Manasa's divinity. Manasa inflicted various calamities upon him, including the loss of his sons and wealth, until he finally relented and worshipped her, thus establishing her veneration among humans. This tale highlights the struggle for recognition and the triumph of a marginalized deity.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Manasa's primary role is to protect her devotees from snakebites and cure snake poisoning. She is particularly important in rural areas where encounters with snakes are frequent. Her worship is also associated with fertility and prosperity. Manasa Puja, dedicated to her, is an important festival, especially in Bengal, where elaborate rituals and offerings are made to appease the goddess. Manasa's rise to prominence reflects the assimilation of local cults into the mainstream Hindu tradition. Her stories often emphasize the power of devotion and the importance of recognizing the divine in all its forms, even those initially rejected or marginalized.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Manasa's association with snakes symbolizes both danger and healing. Snakes are often seen as symbols of death and destruction, but also represent transformation and regeneration due to their shedding of skin. Manasa's control over snakes signifies her ability to overcome these negative aspects and harness their power for good. Her stories can be interpreted as allegories of social struggles and the overcoming of prejudice. Her eventual acceptance represents the integration of marginalized communities and beliefs into the broader religious landscape. The refusal of Chand Sadagar to worship her symbolizes resistance to change and the clinging to traditional norms.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Manasa continues to be widely worshipped in Bengal and other parts of India. Her images and shrines are found in many villages, and her stories are retold in various forms, including folk songs, plays, and visual arts. The Manasa Mangal Kavyas, a genre of Bengali narrative poetry, celebrate her deeds and disseminate her legends. In modern times, Manasa's stories are often reinterpreted to address contemporary social issues, such as gender inequality and social justice. She remains a powerful symbol of resilience, empowerment, and the recognition of marginalized voices.

sources.author: W.J. Wilkins
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Hindu mythology, including accounts of Manasa's origins and legends.

sources.author: Ralph T.H. Griffith
sources.description: While not directly about Manasa, provides context for understanding the Vedic roots of later Hindu deities.

associations.name: Snakes
associations.significance: Snakes are her primary symbol and represent both danger and healing.

associations.name: Poison
associations.significance: She has the power to control and neutralize poison.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Lamashtu
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A female demon associated with disease and danger, but also capable of protection.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Medusa
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Figure associated with snakes; her gaze could turn people to stone, representing a dangerous power.

companions.name: Jaratkaru
companions.description: A sage and her husband, who ultimately rejected her due to his ascetic vows.

companions.name: Astika
companions.description: Her son, who played a role in stopping a great snake sacrifice.

worshipPractices.name: Manasa Puja
worshipPractices.description: Annual worship dedicated to Manasa, involving offerings of flowers, fruits, and milk.

worshipPractices.name: Snake Charming
worshipPractices.description: In some regions, snake charmers are associated with Manasa's worship and are believed to be protected by her.

festivals.name: Naga Panchami
festivals.timing: Shukla Paksha Panchami of Shravana month
festivals.description: A festival dedicated to snake deities, including Manasa, where snakes are worshipped and offered milk.

festivals.name: Manasa Puja (Bengal)
festivals.timing: Varies regionally, often during monsoon season
festivals.description: Specific puja dedicated to Manasa, with elaborate rituals and offerings.

cultural.modernLegacy: Manasa remains a popular deity, particularly in rural Bengal. Her stories continue to be retold and reinterpreted, and her worship provides comfort and protection to her devotees. She also appears in modern art, literature, and film.
